segments.title: Why a child's uniform at Hiroshima stays in memory
segments.summary: Clarke says Hiroshima's museum may be the trip memory that lasts longest because a child's uniform affected him differently from written stories, facts, and figures.
segments.body: At Haneda, Dr. Clarke says the memory most likely to stay with him is Hiroshima and its museum. He contrasts reading stories, facts, and figures with encountering a physical object connected to individual children. The small uniform he recalls turns an abstract historical record into a human-scale experience. His reflection shows how museum objects can carry an emotional and mnemonic force that written information alone may not. This watch point captures a historian considering the difference between knowing the past through records and meeting its surviving material evidence in person.

evidence.note: Clarke says Hiroshima and its museum will haunt his memories, contrasting written stories, facts, and figures with seeing a child's uniform.
